candidate_problems.text: Where is the capability threshold or phase transition at which the bottleneck shifts from substrate to selection architecture, and is this transition sharp, gradual, or task-dependent? The conjecture asserts a regime change but leaves unresolved how to define, measure, or predict it across domains, model classes, and institutional settings.

What concrete selection architectures actually improve truth-tracking under scaling, rather than merely filtering for plausibility, reward-hacking, or institutional conformity? The conjecture elevates criticism, retention, and promotion mechanisms, but the open problem is to specify designs and metrics that distinguish genuine error-correction from selection for persuasive or benchmark-optimized outputs.

Can increased substrate itself generate stronger criticism and selection, collapsing the structure/substrate distinction, or are there irreducibly structural constraints that scaling alone will not solve? This is the deepest unresolved tension in the conjecture: whether selection architecture is an independent bottleneck or just an emergent capability of sufficiently large systems plus compute.
